title: Designing at Scale: Challenges, Pitfalls, and Lessons Learned
summary: This paper discusses the inevitability of architectural decisions in large-scale software systems becoming mistakes over time. It presents real-world examples of design choices that turned into liabilities, identifies recurring patterns of pain, and offers lessons on making conscious trade-offs while minimizing future regrets. The main result is a set of insights for engineers to avoid common pitfalls and leave fewer problematic decisions for successors.
keywords: large-scale systems; architectural decisions; design pitfalls; technical debt; software evolution

title: How Context-Sensitive Static Analysis Led to the Best Smart Contract Decompiler
summary: This paper argues that context-sensitive static analysis is a powerful tool for decompilation, particularly in the domain of Ethereum smart contracts. It presents how such analysis enabled the creation of the most advanced decompiler for Ethereum VM bytecode, framing decompilation as a problem of determining program behavior for all inputs. The talk demonstrates how unusual forms of static analysis can solve this challenge.
keywords: static analysis; decompilation; smart contracts; Ethereum VM; context-sensitive analysis

title: Find My Code Twin: Improving SNIPPET SEARCH Performance Using LLMs in Practice
summary: This paper describes SNIPPET SEARCH, a code clone detection and snippet search framework deployed at Samsung Electronics that uses a two-stage approach combining LLM-based summarization with token-level similarity refinement. The system achieves high performance across multiple programming languages, improving F1 scores by 21-28% over existing methods, and improves test case quality by at least 20% in embedded systems projects.
keywords: code clone detection; snippet search; LLM summarization; enterprise software; multilingual code search

title: R-Log: Incentivizing Log Analysis Capability in LLMs via Reasoning-based Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper introduces R-Log, a method that trains large language models to analyze software system logs more effectively by first teaching them structured reasoning strategies based on human expert practices, then refining their performance using reinforcement learning in a simulated operations environment. The main result is that R-Log significantly outperforms existing approaches across five log analysis tasks, especially on unseen data (improving accuracy by 228%), and a faster variant achieves 5x speedup while retaining 93% of the effectiveness.
keywords: log analysis; reasoning-based reinforcement learning; large language models; operations and maintenance (O&M); hallucination reduction

title: PerFrame: Monitoring GUI Loading Performance in Mobile Apps via Semantic Distinguish
summary: PerFrame is a framework that uses a multimodal large language model to automatically monitor GUI loading performance in mobile apps by identifying start and end frames from screencasts. It outperforms previous methods, achieving less than 5-frame discrepancy in 61.6% of test cases and a 27.7% improvement over prior approaches. This automation reduces manual effort and accelerates testing efficiency for app reliability.
keywords: GUI loading performance; multimodal large language model; mobile app testing; UI animation handling; semantic frame distinction

title: Automated Software Test Generation at Industry Scale Using a Multi-Agent Architecture and Workflow Integration
summary: This paper presents AutoCover, a production system that uses large language models and a multi-agent architecture to automatically generate, validate, and propose software tests at industry scale. The system supports three interaction modes (CLI, Headless, and IDE) and includes validation gates like coverage checks and flakiness defenses. Its main result is that AutoCover now generates about 11% of all new tests reviewed and added to the company's codebase.
keywords: automated test generation; multi-agent architecture; large language models; workflow integration; coverage improvement

title: Learning without Forgetting: Towards Continual learning of Fault Localization Models in Industrial Software Systems
summary: The paper presents Canto, a continual learning framework for fault localization in large-scale industrial software systems that enables models to learn new faults without forgetting old ones. By extracting fine-grained semantics from logs and using unsupervised clustering to select representative examples, Canto significantly outperforms existing continual learning methods, improving effectiveness by 17-45%.
keywords: continual learning; fault localization; software system testing; catastrophic forgetting; industrial software

title: Memory-Efficient Large Language Models for Program Repair with Semantic-Guided Patch Generation
summary: This paper introduces FLAMES, a memory-efficient LLM-based automated program repair method that replaces memory-heavy beam search with greedy decoding guided by semantic feedback from test validation. FLAMES reduces GPU memory usage by up to 83% while fixing more bugs (133 on Defects4J) and improving patch generation on other benchmarks compared to existing approaches.
keywords: automated program repair; large language models; memory efficiency; semantic-guided patch generation; greedy decoding

title: Func: Reducing the impact of Android framework evolution on malware detection
summary: This paper addresses how Android framework updates cause APIs and permissions to change, harming malware detection accuracy. The authors propose a new robust representation (Func) that maps deprecated and replacement APIs/permissions, using an LLM to find replacements when documentation is missing. Experiments show that malware detectors using Func features achieve significantly better F1 scores across Android API levels 9-24.
keywords: Android malware detection; framework evolution; API deprecation; permission changes; LLM-assisted mining

title: Diffploit: Facilitating Cross-Version Exploit Migration for Open Source Library Vulnerabilities
summary: Diffploit is a tool that automatically migrates exploits for open-source library vulnerabilities across different software versions by analyzing behavioral differences and using LLM-guided adaptation. It outperforms existing tools by successfully migrating 84.2% of exploits and has discovered previously unknown vulnerable versions and incorrect vulnerability reports.
keywords: exploit migration; vulnerability detection; LLM-based adaptation; software evolution; open-source libraries

title: ViTAL: LLM-Powered Taint Analysis for GUI Field Visualization Auditing in Android
summary: ViTAL is a framework that combines static analysis with large language models to audit whether server-side data fields in Android apps are displayed on the user interface. It achieves 93% recall and 79% precision, successfully uncovering previously missed visualization fields. The tool helps improve security auditing of user-generated content in mobile apps.
keywords: taint analysis; Android security; large language models; GUI visualization auditing; static analysis

title: LLM Test Generation via Iterative Hybrid Program Analysis
summary: Panta is a technique that combines static analysis and dynamic code coverage feedback to guide LLMs in generating unit tests with higher coverage. It iteratively analyzes control flow and execution paths to help LLMs produce tests for previously uncovered branches and lines. The method achieves 26% higher line coverage and 23% higher branch coverage over existing approaches.
keywords: test generation; large language models; static analysis; code coverage; iterative feedback

title: SEER: Enhancing Chain-of-Thought Code Generation through Self-Exploring Deep Reasoning
summary: This paper introduces SEER, a framework that treats Chain-of-Thought code generation as a decision-making problem to improve LLM-based code generation. It enhances reasoning by exploring diverse paths, assessing step quality with a value model, and dynamically switching between direct and step-by-step approaches. Experiments show SEER significantly outperforms baselines on benchmarks like MBPP and HumanEval.
keywords: code generation; Chain-of-Thought reasoning; Large Language Models; decision making; deep reasoning

title: Green Software Engineering in Practice: Quantifying the Impact of Carbon Reduction Strategies in Enterprise Software
summary: This paper applies the Software Carbon Intensity (SCI) specification to quantify the carbon footprint of an enterprise financial application and tests 18 application-level configurations. The main result is that garbage collection optimization reduced carbon emissions by up to 30.6%, while other configurations had minimal effect. The study demonstrates that developers can achieve significant carbon reductions through application-level adjustments without compromising functionality.
keywords: green software engineering; software carbon intensity; carbon footprint reduction; application-level optimization; enterprise software

title: PerfScout: An Adaptive Workload Generator in Software Performance Testing
summary: PerfScout is a reinforcement learning-based framework that automates software performance testing by adaptively generating workloads instead of using static rules. It identifies system breaking points with high accuracy and improves testing efficiency by up to 90% in real-world deployments at Huawei Cloud.
keywords: adaptive workload generation; reinforcement learning; performance testing; breaking point identification; PPO

title: NotDec: WebAssembly Decompilation With Inter-Procedural Type Recovery
summary: This paper presents NotDec, a WebAssembly decompilation framework that improves type recovery and readability of decompiled C code. It achieves 100% recompilation success on thousands of test samples and recovers over 85% of struct member accesses in real-world programs, significantly outperforming existing tools like Ghidra.
keywords: WebAssembly; decompilation; type recovery; inter-procedural analysis; binary auditing

title: Why Attention Fails: A Taxonomy of Faults in Attention-Based Neural Networks
summary: This paper presents the first comprehensive empirical study of faults in attention-based neural networks, analyzing 555 real-world faults from 96 projects. It develops a novel taxonomy of seven attention-specific fault categories not covered by existing taxonomies, and identifies diagnostic heuristics that explain 33% of these faults. The main result is that over half of all faults in such networks stem from mechanisms unique to attention architectures.
keywords: attention mechanisms; neural network faults; taxonomy; empirical study; diagnostic heuristics

title: LogFold: Compressing Logs with Structured Tokens and Hybrid Encoding
summary: This paper introduces LogFold, a new method for compressing log files that identifies redundancies in structured tokens and uses a type-aware hybrid encoding strategy. It achieves an average 11.11% improvement in compression ratio over existing state-of-the-art methods across 16 public datasets. The method consists of four components: a token analyzer, a pattern miner for structured tokens, a hybrid encoder, and a packer.
keywords: log compression; structured tokens; hybrid encoding; token classification; data compression

title: IEEE Computer Society Harlan D. Mills Award and Talk by Andreas Zeller: Should Computer Scientists Experiment Less? On the past, present, and future of software engineering research
summary: This paper critiques the current emphasis on experimental evaluation in software engineering research, arguing that it has shifted from a quality filter to a competition for better benchmark numbers. The author predicts that AI agents will soon automate experimentation and paper writing, and proposes that researchers should refocus on uniquely human strengths like creativity, abstraction, and normativity. The main result is a call to rethink the role of researchers and prioritize human-centric contributions over automated optimizations.
keywords: software engineering research; experimental evaluation; AI agents; benchmark competition; human-centric research

title: WhatsCode: Large-Scale GenAI Deployment for Developer Efficiency at WhatsApp
summary: This paper presents WhatsCode, a domain-specific AI system deployed at WhatsApp to improve developer efficiency over 25 months. It evolved from privacy automation to autonomous agentic workflows, achieving significant impact such as tripling privacy verification coverage and generating thousands of accepted code changes. The study highlights that organizational factors and human-AI collaboration patterns are key to success in large-scale, compliance-relevant environments.
keywords: AI-assisted development; agentic workflows; developer efficiency; human-AI collaboration; enterprise AI deployment

title: Assessing Coherency and Consistency of Code Execution Reasoning by Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces CES, a task to evaluate how well LLMs simulate program execution and use that reasoning in programming tasks. It measures coherence—whether the simulation follows commonsense logic even when predictions are wrong—and consistency across tests. The main finding is that even frontier LLMs like GPT-4 often rely on shortcuts rather than genuine execution reasoning, and their reasoning is mostly inconsistent, limiting their reliability for path-sensitive programming tasks.
keywords: LLM reasoning; program execution simulation; coherence metric; consistency evaluation; code understanding

title: Top General Performance = Top Domain Performance? DomainCodeBench: A Multi-domain Code Generation Benchmark
summary: This paper introduces DomainCodeBench, a multi-domain benchmark for evaluating code generation in large language models across 12 application domains and 15 programming languages. The main finding is that top general-domain models do not consistently perform well in specialized domains, and that adding domain-specific knowledge to prompts can improve performance by about 38%.
keywords: code generation; large language models; domain-specific benchmark; performance decoupling; domain knowledge

title: The SWE-Bench Illusion: When State-of-the-Art LLMs Remember Instead of Reason
summary: This paper investigates whether state-of-the-art LLMs truly reason or simply memorize when solving software engineering tasks on the SWE-Bench Verified benchmark. The authors introduce diagnostic tests to detect memorization and find that models achieve high accuracy on SWE-Bench but much lower accuracy on similar tasks from other repositories, suggesting data contamination or memorization. The main result is that performance gains on SWE-Bench may be inflated by memorization rather than genuine problem-solving ability.
keywords: memorization; LLM evaluation; benchmark contamination; software engineering; SWE-Bench

title: What’s DAT? Three Case Studies of Measuring Software Development Productivity at Meta With Diff Authoring Time
summary: This paper introduces Diff Authoring Time (DAT), a time-based metric for measuring software development productivity by tracking how long engineers take to create self-contained code changes. The authors validate DAT at Meta through experiments and case studies, showing improvements such as a 14% gain from typed mocks and 33% from automatic memoization. DAT enables rigorous, experiment-driven development workflows for internal tools.
keywords: Diff Authoring Time; software development productivity; time-based metric; Meta; experimentation

title: Hallucinating Certificates: Differential Testing of TLS Certificate Validation Using Generative Language Models
summary: This paper presents MLCerts, a new approach that uses generative language models to create synthetic certificates for differential testing of TLS certificate validation. The main result is that MLCerts discovers significantly more discrepancies between five major TLS implementations than prior methods, revealing new security-relevant bugs.
keywords: TLS certificate validation; differential testing; generative language models; security bugs; X.509 certificates

title: Fuzzing Java Optimizing Compilers with Complex Inter-Class Structures Guided by Heterogeneous Program Graphs
summary: This paper presents InterFuzz, a fuzzing framework for testing Java compilers by generating test cases with complex inter-class structures. It uses a Heterogeneous Program Graph (HPG) to model and manipulate these relationships, and successfully uncovered 24 bugs in production compilers, most of which depend on intricate inter-class interactions. The main result is that InterFuzz finds bugs missed by traditional fuzzers, demonstrating the importance of testing inter-class structures.
keywords: fuzzing; Java compilers; inter-class structures; heterogeneous program graph; compiler bugs

title: META²V2V: Revealing Behavioural Deviations under Mutual Perception in Multi-Vehicle Autonomous Driving
summary: This paper introduces META²V2V, a metamorphic testing framework for evaluating autonomous driving systems (ADS) in multi-vehicle scenarios where vehicles share information via V2V communication. The framework injects controlled perturbations into V2V data to test the soundness and robustness of ADS decisions, using multi-objective search to efficiently generate test cases. Experiments show META²V2V identifies significantly more behavioral deviations than random testing.
keywords: autonomous driving; vehicle-to-vehicle communication; metamorphic testing; multi-vehicle systems; behavioral deviations

title: Beyond the Commit: Developer Perspectives on Productivity with AI Coding Assistants
summary: This paper investigates how to measure the productivity impact of AI coding assistants on developers, using a mixed-method study with surveys and interviews at BNY Mellon. It finds that conflicting survey opinions and six distinct factors—including long-term dimensions like technical expertise and work ownership—show a multifaceted approach is needed. The main result is that traditional short-term metrics are insufficient, and human-centered long-term factors are crucial for evaluating AI's effect on developer productivity.
keywords: developer productivity; AI coding assistants; mixed-method study; productivity measurement; human-centered metrics

title: Assessing the Sustainability-Quality (SQ) Model: A Multi-Year Empirical Study in Education
summary: This paper presents a multi-year empirical study where master's students applied the Sustainability-Quality (SQ) Model to 63 projects across various domains, collecting 275 sustainability concerns. The results show that while the model achieved 88% classification accuracy, concerns like carbon footprint were less frequently identified, indicating usability and areas for improvement in sustainability education. The study provides evidence that the SQ Model can reliably help novices connect quality attributes to sustainability dimensions.
keywords: Sustainability-Quality Model; software engineering; sustainability assessment; quality attributes; education

title: Fairness-First Design Thinking for Software Architecture
summary: This paper introduces a fairness-first Design Thinking approach for software architecture to address fairness issues in digital systems. The authors implemented this approach in a graduate course and analyzed student assignments to derive implications for practice and education. Their main result shows that fairness theory and context identification are essential for holistic design, and they propose using composite views to handle fairness as a cross-cutting concern.
keywords: fairness-first design; software architecture; Design Thinking; fairness in AI; software engineering education

title: Enforcing Control Flow Integrity on DeFi Smart Contracts
summary: This paper presents CrossGuard, a framework that enforces control flow integrity on DeFi smart contracts to detect and block attacks in real time. By analyzing historical transactions, the authors found that attacks introduce novel control flows, so CrossGuard uses whitelisting to revert transactions that deviate from observed benign patterns. The system blocks 35 out of 37 attacks with a low false positive rate and minimal cost, improving security without needing prior knowledge of specific exploits.
keywords: control flow integrity; DeFi security; smart contract protection; real-time attack detection; whitelisting policies

title: AnchorDiff: Binary OSS Version Identification Method Based on Anchor Node Slicing
summary: This paper presents AnchorDiff, a method for identifying the exact version of binary open-source software used in firmware by partitioning the version chain into regions using anchor nodes and performing binary code similarity detection. The main result is that AnchorDiff achieves high accuracy (up to 97.17%) while reducing storage requirements by a factor of 36, outperforming current state-of-the-art approaches.
keywords: binary code similarity; version identification; open-source software; security vulnerabilities; firmware analysis

title: Generating Energy-Efficient Code via Large-Language Models - Where are we now?
summary: This paper evaluates the energy efficiency of Python code generated by large language models (LLMs) compared to human-written code and code from a green software expert. Across 363 solutions and three hardware platforms, human-written code was more efficient on a server and Raspberry Pi, while LLMs performed best on a PC, but no LLM matched the efficiency of an experienced green software developer. The findings highlight that human expertise remains crucial for writing energy-efficient code.
keywords: energy efficiency; LLM-generated code; Python; green software; benchmarking

title: Depradar: Agentic Coordination for Context-Aware Defect Impact Analysis in Deep Learning Libraries
summary: DepRadar is a framework that uses four specialized AI agents to analyze defects introduced by updates to deep learning libraries like Transformers and Megatron. It extracts defect semantics from code changes, synthesizes triggering conditions, and checks downstream programs for impact, achieving high precision and recall. The main result is that DepRadar effectively identifies affected client code and silent defects, outperforming existing baselines.
keywords: agent coordination; defect impact analysis; deep learning libraries; static analysis; software maintenance

title: Intelligent Triage: Interpretable Incident Triage Workflow using LLM Extracted Triage Reasoning
summary: This paper introduces Intelligent Triage, a workflow that uses LLMs to extract step-wise triage reasoning from past incident solutions, rather than relying on end-to-end retrieval. The approach improves accuracy by 10 percentage points and reduces time to mitigation (TTM) by providing interpretable justifications for upstream tools and downstream teams. It has been deployed in Microsoft Azure for over six months with strong offline and online evaluation results.
keywords: incident triage; LLM reasoning; explainable AI; cloud services; workflow automation

title: TerraFormer: Automated Infrastructure-as-Code with LLMs Fine-Tuned via Policy-Guided Verifier Feedback
summary: TerraFormer is a framework that uses large language models fine-tuned with verifier feedback to automatically generate correct Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) configurations from natural language. It combines supervised learning with reinforcement learning guided by formal tools that check syntax, deployability, and policy compliance, and outperforms many larger models on IaC generation and mutation tasks. The main result is significant improvements in correctness and compliance compared to existing LLMs.
keywords: Infrastructure-as-Code; LLM fine-tuning; automated configuration generation; policy compliance; neuro-symbolic framework

title: Scalpel: Automotive Deep Learning Framework Testing via Assembling Model Components
summary: This paper presents Scalpel, a method for testing automotive deep learning frameworks by assembling model components (heads, necks, backbones) to create test inputs that support multi-input, multi-modal, and multi-level processing. Scalpel detects crashes and bugs in frameworks like PaddleInference, achieving significant improvements in model generation and bug detection efficiency. The main result is that Scalpel found 16 crashes and 21 NaN & inconsistency bugs in Apollo, with 10 crashes confirmed by the community.
keywords: deep learning testing; autonomous driving; model component assembly; automotive framework; bug detection

title: CombCT: Compiler Testing via Combinatorial Testing
summary: This paper introduces CombCT, a novel compiler testing approach that uses combinatorial testing to systematically explore vast optimization sequences, which are a primary source of compiler bugs. The method incorporates techniques like dynamic sequence adaptation and diversity augmentation to improve bug detection. Experiments show CombCT finds more unique bugs than current methods, and it has already uncovered 14 previously unknown bugs in GCC and LLVM, most of which have been confirmed or fixed.
keywords: compiler testing; combinatorial testing; optimization sequences; bug detection; GCC and LLVM

title: GUISpector: An MLLM Agent Framework for Automated Verification of Natural Language Requirements in GUI Prototypes
summary: GUISpector is a framework that uses a multimodal large language model (MLLM) agent to automatically verify whether GUI prototypes meet natural language requirements. The agent plans and executes verification steps, provides detailed feedback for developers, and integrates with LLM-driven code generation to create a closed-loop improvement process. Evaluated on 150 requirements, it effectively detects satisfaction and violations.
keywords: GUI verification; multimodal LLM agent; natural language requirements; automated testing; agentic AI

title: GPTrace: Effective Crash Deduplication Using LLM Embeddings
summary: GPTrace uses a large language model to generate embeddings from crash-related data (e.g., stack traces) and then clusters them to deduplicate crashing inputs from fuzzing campaigns. The approach significantly improves deduplication accuracy over traditional hand-crafted methods and more complex state-of-the-art techniques, evaluated on over 300,000 crashes across 14 targets. This reduces manual analysis effort by grouping crashes that share the same underlying bug.
keywords: crash deduplication; fuzzing; LLM embeddings; software vulnerability; clustering

title: StorFuzz: Using Data Diversity to Overcome Fuzzing Plateaus
summary: StorFuzz is a new fuzzing technique that overcomes plateaus in long-running fuzzing campaigns by using data coverage based on memory stores, rather than relying solely on code coverage. It diversifies the corpus to discover new program behavior and bugs, finding 50 new bugs in well-tested projects like VLC and PHP. The method outperforms existing fuzzers on multiple benchmarks and is complementary to static data-guided approaches.
keywords: fuzzing; data coverage; memory stores; bug discovery; software testing

title: Knowledge Matters: Injecting Project and Testing Knowledge into LLM-based Unit Test Generation
summary: This paper introduces KTester, a framework that improves LLM-based unit test generation by injecting project-specific and testing-domain knowledge through static analysis and structured prompting. The approach separates test design from test generation and uses multi-perspective heuristics, outperforming existing methods in correctness, coverage, and maintainability across multiple open-source projects.
keywords: unit test generation; large language models; knowledge injection; static analysis; software testing

title: Towards Bridging Language Gaps in OSS with LLM-Driven Documentation Translation
summary: This paper explores using large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT and Claude to translate open-source technical documentation, which mixes natural language, code, and markdown. It finds that LLMs can produce accurate semantic translations but sometimes fail to preserve formatting and technical details. The study provides a foundation for automated, LLM-driven documentation translation in open-source projects.
keywords: large language models; documentation translation; open source software; technical documentation; automated translation

title: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Secret Key Leakage in Hugging Face Spaces
summary: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of secret key leakage in Hugging Face Spaces, analyzing over 300,000 public repositories. The authors developed a framework called Secret Reviewer that combines static analysis and LLM-based detection, finding nearly 10,000 repositories with leaked secrets, including many from major AI providers, with a significant portion remaining active. The study highlights critical security risks in AI platform ecosystems and calls for stronger security practices.
keywords: secret leakage; Hugging Face Spaces; API token exposure; static analysis; LLM-assisted detection

title: How Good are Input Grammar Miners? An Empirical Study
summary: This paper evaluates the accuracy of input grammar miners, which extract context-free grammars from inputs or code to generate valid test inputs. The authors find that previous evaluations are biased towards short inputs and propose using k-path coverage to measure grammar diversity at different depths. Their results show that precision and recall drop significantly for deeper diversity, especially for complex languages, indicating that existing grammar miners need improvement.
keywords: grammar mining; empirical evaluation; test input generation; context-free grammar; k-path coverage

title: Catching License Changes Before They Cost: Leveraging LLMs to Analyze License Evolution in Open Source Software - Solution to challenge #13
summary: This paper proposes using large language models (LLMs) to detect and analyze license changes in open-source software over time, aiming to prevent costly compliance issues. The main result is a solution to a specific challenge that leverages LLMs for automated license evolution analysis.
keywords: license evolution; open source software; LLMs; license compliance; software licensing

title: SEALing the Gap: A Reference Framework for LLM Inference Carbon Estimation via Multi-Benchmark Driven Embodiment
summary: This paper proposes a reference framework called SEAL for estimating the carbon footprint of LLM inference at the per-prompt level, addressing the growing sustainability concerns of LLMs in software engineering. The framework uses multi-benchmark-driven machine learning models to provide accurate carbon measurements, with initial validation showing promising results. The work aims to standardize sustainability assessment across the LLM ecosystem.
keywords: LLM inference carbon estimation; sustainability assessment; carbon footprint; machine learning models; green AI

title: Sapling: Quantifying and Measuring the Maturity of the RISC-V Software Ecosystem
summary: This paper introduces Sapling, an automated framework that evaluates the maturity of the RISC-V software ecosystem across multiple domains, components, and dimensions. The study finds that the ecosystem is still developing but shows steady progress and potential for broader industry use, and it provides key insights and recommendations for further advancement.
keywords: RISC-V; software ecosystem maturity; open-source hardware; automated evaluation framework; ecosystem assessment

title: No Shot in the Dark: Efficient Context-Free Language Reachability via Context-Aware Tabulation
summary: This paper introduces context-aware tabulation (CAT), a method that adds limited context sensitivity to context-free language reachability to prune unproductive edges during static program analysis. Experiments show CAT achieves up to 2.13x speedup and reduces memory usage by up to 50% compared to existing solvers.
keywords: context-free language reachability; static program analysis; context-aware tabulation; edge pruning; performance optimization

title: SSAR: A Novel Software Architecture Recovery Approach Enhancing Accuracy and Scalability
summary: This paper presents a new software architecture recovery approach called SSAR that combines semantic similarity and structural dependencies between files to create a weighted graph, then uses an optimized community detection algorithm to partition the graph for modularization. The method improves accuracy by up to 500% on certain metrics and reduces execution time by 5% to 99% compared to existing techniques. The main result is a more precise and scalable architecture recovery process for large-scale software systems.
keywords: software architecture recovery; community detection; modularization; weighted graph; accuracy and scalability

title: An Ontology-Based Approach to Security Risk Identification for Container Deployments in OT Contexts
summary: This paper presents a model-based approach called the Container Security Risk Ontology (CSRO) to automate security risk identification for container deployments in operational technology (OT) environments. It integrates key domains such as adversarial behavior, attack scenarios, and risk assessment rules to enable reproducible and interpretable risk analysis. The approach is validated through a case study, showing that it can automatically calculate risk levels from deployment artifacts.
keywords: container security; operational technology; risk identification; ontology; automated risk assessment

title: EmbedAgent: Benchmarking Large Language Models in Embedded System Development
summary: This paper introduces EmbedAgent, a benchmark for evaluating large language models on embedded system development tasks, including programming, circuit design, and cross-platform migration. It tests ten LLMs across 126 cases and finds that even top models like DeepSeek-R1 struggle, achieving only 55.6% accuracy with schematics and 50% without. The authors also propose strategies like retrieval-augmented generation and compiler feedback, which improve performance significantly.
keywords: large language models; embedded systems; benchmark; circuit design; cross-platform migration

title: The Undecidability of Overfitting in Automated Program Repair
summary: This paper argues that overfitting in automated program repair (APR) is not merely a practical flaw but a fundamental consequence of undecidability, arising from the inherent incompleteness of test suites as correctness oracles. The authors reframe overfitting as an unavoidable structural boundary of APR, rather than a solvable issue, and propose a research agenda focused on quantifying, explaining, and reasoning about overfitting within these limits.
keywords: automated program repair; overfitting; undecidability; test suites; software correctness

title: Debugging Performance Issues in WebAssembly Runtimes via Mutation-based Inference
summary: This paper introduces WarpL, a mutation-based tool for debugging performance issues in WebAssembly (Wasm) runtimes by identifying suboptimal instruction sequences. The tool creates functionally similar mutants where the issue disappears and compares machine code to pinpoint root causes. Evaluated on 12 real-world issues, WarpL correctly identified causes in 10 cases and discovered six previously unknown issues in Wasmtime.
keywords: WebAssembly; performance debugging; mutation-based analysis; runtime optimization; Wasmtime

title: An Eye for AI: Eye-Tracking the Micro-Interruptions of GenAI Code Suggestions
summary: This paper uses eye-tracking to study how developers interact with generative AI code suggestions, finding that about half of suggestions are not looked at and over 75% of those viewed are not used. These suggestions cause brief micro-interruptions that disrupt developer flow, even though they are reviewed for less than a second on average. The authors propose more efficient and context-aware AI suggestions to reduce cognitive overhead.
keywords: eye-tracking; generative AI; code suggestions; developer cognition; micro-interruptions

title: Inside Out: Uncovering How Comment Internalization Steers LLMs for Better or Worse
summary: This paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) internalize comments in source code during software engineering tasks. By using Concept Activation Vectors, the authors show that LLMs treat comments as distinct concepts and that manipulating these concepts can drastically change task performance. The main result is that code summarization strongly activates comment concepts, while code completion shows weak sensitivity, suggesting new ways to build SE tools that reason about internal representations.
keywords: concept interpretability; LLM; code comments; software engineering; Concept Activation Vectors

title: A First Look at Model Supply Chain: From the Risk Perspective
summary: This paper constructs the first comprehensive model supply chain map for AI models on Hugging Face, analyzing over 1.8 million models and half a million dependency relationships. It characterizes the usage, evolution, quality, and risks of this supply chain, offering insights for stakeholders to improve model reuse and risk management.
keywords: model supply chain; Hugging Face; risk management; AI model provenance; model dependency

title: Exploring and Improving Real-World Vulnerability Data Generation via Prompting Large Language Models
summary: This paper explores using large language models (LLMs) to generate realistic vulnerability data for software security analysis, finding that advanced prompting strategies like few-shot learning and VICS achieve up to 93% success on synthetic data and 88% on real-world code. The generated data significantly improves downstream deep learning models for vulnerability analysis, boosting accuracy by up to 36.2% and even outperforming advanced LLMs directly performing the analysis. The main result is that LLM-generated vulnerability data is highly useful for training better security models.
keywords: vulnerability generation; large language models; prompt engineering; software security; data augmentation

title: Project-Level Resource Leak Detection through Agent-based Ownership Analysis and Repair Pattern Verification
summary: This paper introduces AROP, a framework for detecting resource leaks in large codebases by combining LLM-based agents with project-level metadata analysis. It achieves high precision and recall on benchmarks, discovering over 100 new real-world defects, with many patches accepted by developers.
keywords: resource leak detection; LLM-based agents; static analysis; project-level metadata; data-flow analysis

title: Understanding DevOps Security of Google Workspace Apps
summary: This paper investigates the DevOps security of Google Workspace add-ons, focusing on how third-party apps are developed, deployed, and operated. The authors propose a hybrid static and dynamic analysis method to uncover vulnerabilities such as source code leakage and secret key exposure. Their large-scale study of 5,300 add-ons found that 274 apps leaked source code, and 96 of those exposed sensitive developer secrets like database or payment keys.
keywords: DevOps security; Google Workspace add-ons; source code leakage; secret key exposure; third-party app vulnerabilities

title: Towards Automating Domain-Specific Data Generation for Text-to-SQL: A Comprehensive Approach
summary: This paper presents SelectCraft, a method for automatically generating large-scale, domain-specific Text-to-SQL datasets by leveraging existing database structures. As a proof of concept, the authors created a financial dataset with over one million samples and fine-tuned a new language model, BanQL, which outperformed existing models in accuracy and generalizability. The work aims to improve the reliability of natural language interfaces that convert text to SQL queries.
keywords: Text-to-SQL; domain-specific data generation; large language models; automatic dataset creation; natural language interfaces

title: Towards Scalable and Interpretable Mobile App Risk Analysis via Large Language Models
summary: This paper presents Mars, a system that uses large language models (LLMs) to automate the analysis of mobile apps for security risks, reducing the need for manual review. It filters app features through a risk identification tree before LLM analysis to improve accuracy, and automatically generates evidence chains for transparency. Evaluated on real-world Android app data, Mars achieved high F1-scores in risk identification and evidence retrieval, and a user study showed it improved analyst efficiency by 60-90%.
keywords: mobile app security; LLM; risk analysis; automated vetting; evidence chain

title: Environment-Aware Code Generation: How far are We?
summary: This paper introduces Environment-Aware Code Generation (EACG), which requires LLMs to produce code that is both functionally correct and executable under specific user software environments. The authors create a benchmark called VersiBCB from real-world Python projects and test three adaptation strategies (retrieval-augmented, mixture-of-experts, memory-augmented). Their main result is that current LLMs struggle with environment-specific code generation, but their strategies improve compatibility and executability.
keywords: environment-aware code generation; large language models; software environment compatibility; benchmark construction; code generation adaptation

title: NB2P: Generating Data Science Pipelines from Computational Notebooks
summary: This paper presents NB2P, a system that automatically extracts data science pipelines from computational notebooks. By parsing notebooks into abstract syntax trees and using a learned encoding-decoding algorithm for semantic segmentation, NB2P recovers execution order and groups operations into pipeline stages. Experiments show it outperforms baselines, including large language models, for extracting deployable pipelines.
keywords: computational notebooks; data science pipelines; semantic segmentation; abstract syntax tree; pipeline extraction

title: Characterizing Regression Bug‑Inducing Changes and Improving LLM‑Based Regression Bug Detection
summary: This paper characterizes the code changes that introduce regression bugs by analyzing 280 bugs from open-source Java projects, finding that feature additions, bug fixes, performance improvements, and refactoring are common triggers. It also evaluates large language models (LLMs) for detecting such bugs, showing limited ability, and proposes LLM4Reg, a method that significantly improves precision, recall, and explanation in LLM-based regression bug detection.
keywords: regression bugs; bug-inducing changes; large language models; software quality; Java projects

title: Model Discovery and Graph Simulation: A Lightweight Gateway to Chaos Engineering
summary: This paper proposes a lightweight approach to chaos engineering by using a simple service-dependency graph model with replica counts to estimate system availability under fail-stop faults. The authors introduce model discovery, an automated method that builds such models from existing artifacts like distributed traces and service-mesh telemetry, eliminating the need for handcrafted models. Experiments on DeathStarBench show the discovered model closely tracks live fault-injection results, offering a low-cost way to triage expensive chaos experiments and generate real-time resilience signals.
keywords: chaos engineering; model discovery; service-dependency graph; availability estimation; resilience testing

title: Unveiling the Potential of Diffusion Large Language Models in Software Engineering Tasks: An Empirical Study
summary: This paper evaluates Diffusion Large Language Models (DLLMs) across software engineering tasks like code generation, defect detection, and program repair, comparing them to autoregressive LLMs. On a large benchmark of 52,937 tasks, 7B-parameter DLLMs achieved a 30% average accuracy improvement over AR-LLMs, with 113% gain on cross-file repair and better efficiency. The results suggest DLLMs are a superior paradigm for software engineering tasks.
keywords: Diffusion Large Language Models; software engineering; code generation; defect detection; program repair

title: MAJIT: Just-in-Time Detection of Compatibility Issues in Android and iOS Apps through Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: This paper presents MAJIT, a multi-agent system using large language models to detect compatibility issues in Android and iOS apps just-in-time during code changes. It achieves over 96% precision and recall for system version, device, and screen size compatibility issues.
keywords: multi-agent system; compatibility detection; Android; iOS; large language model

title: Atomizer: An LLM-based Collaborative Multi-Agent Framework for Intent-Driven Commit Untangling
summary: Atomizer is a multi-agent framework that uses large language models to untangle composite commits in software development by inferring the semantic intent of code changes. It employs an Intent-Oriented Chain-of-Thought strategy and a grouper-reviewer refinement loop to iteratively improve groupings. The method outperforms existing graph-based approaches, especially on complex commits, by over 6% on C# and 5.5% on Java datasets.
keywords: commit untangling; multi-agent framework; large language models; semantic intent; software maintenance

title: ConfLogger: Enhance Systems' Configuration Diagnosability through Configuration Logging
summary: This paper introduces ConfLogger, a tool that improves the diagnosability of configuration-related issues in software systems by combining static taint analysis with LLM-based log generation. It automatically identifies configuration-sensitive code and generates diagnostic log statements, achieving 100% accuracy in error localization for silent misconfigurations and significantly outperforming existing methods in logging coverage and diagnostic value.
keywords: configuration logging; software diagnosability; static taint analysis; LLM-based log generation; misconfiguration detection

title: Context-Free Grammar Inference for Complex Programming Languages in Black Box Settings
summary: This paper presents Crucio, a new method for inferring context-free grammars for complex programming languages like C, C++, and Java, where existing tools fail to finish within 48 hours. Crucio uses a decomposition forest and distributional matrix to efficiently extract short examples for lexical and grammar inference, achieving successful grammar inference with up to 17× more nonterminals than prior benchmarks and improving recall and F1 scores over previous methods.
keywords: grammar inference; context-free grammar; programming languages; black box learning; decomposition forest

title: LoopSCC: Summarizing Complex Multi-branch Nested Loops via Periodic Oscillation Interval
summary: This paper presents LoopSCC, a tool that summarizes complex multi-branch nested loops by constructing an SPath Graph and collapsing strongly connected components into acyclic graphs. It introduces oscillation intervals to model irregular jumps between loop branches, enabling precise summarization of loop behavior for symbolic analysis. The tool achieves 100% precision on various loop types and discovered 18 new vulnerabilities in IoT projects.
keywords: loop summarization; symbolic execution; static analysis; control flow complexity; vulnerability detection

title: Efficient Strong Updates For Path Sensitive Data Dependence Analysis
summary: This paper improves the efficiency of path-sensitive data dependence analysis, which is used for static vulnerability detection. It speeds up the process by handling most cases with efficient must-kill relation inference and reserving complex path-sensitive analysis for difficult cases. The main result is significant speed-ups and improved state coverage in static analysis.
keywords: data dependence analysis; path-sensitive analysis; strong updates; static vulnerability detection; load-store matching

title: Change And Cover: Last-Mile, Pull Request-Based Regression Test Augmentation
summary: This paper presents ChaCo, an LLM-based tool that automatically generates regression tests for code changed in pull requests, targeting the 'last-mile' gap where modified lines remain untested. It extracts relevant test context, generates well-integrated tests, and achieves full patch coverage for 30% of PRs at low cost. Human reviewers rated the tests highly, and several submitted tests were merged, including two that uncovered previously unknown bugs.
keywords: regression test augmentation; pull request testing; LLM-based test generation; patch coverage; continuous integration

title: Relax with Capybaras
summary: This is a placeholder slot in a conference program, offering attendees capybara-themed relaxation instead of a technical presentation. It has no research content or results.
keywords: presentation slot; conference program; capybara; relaxation; empty slot

title: One Signature, Multiple Payments: Demystifying and Detecting Signature Replay Vulnerabilities in Smart Contracts
summary: This paper investigates signature replay vulnerabilities in smart contracts, where missing checks on signature usage conditions allow repeated verifications leading to permission abuse and asset loss. The authors develop LASiR, a tool combining LLM-based semantic analysis with static taint analysis and symbolic execution to automatically detect such vulnerabilities. In a large-scale study across four blockchains, they find SRVs are widespread, affecting contracts holding $4.76 million in active assets, and LASiR achieves an F1-score of 87.90%.
keywords: smart contracts; signature replay vulnerability; LLM-assisted detection; static taint analysis; blockchain security

title: USCSA: Evolution-Aware Security Analysis for Proxy-Based Upgradeable Smart Contracts
summary: This paper introduces USCSA, a tool that analyzes security risks in upgradeable smart contracts by comparing code changes across versions using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) differences and large language models (LLMs). It detects vulnerabilities like reentrancy and access control flaws with high precision (92.26%) and recall (89.67%). The main result is a novel solution for auditing blockchain contract upgrades.
keywords: smart contract; blockchain security; upgradeable contracts; vulnerability detection; AST analysis
